text: The surgeon begins posterior dissection between the bladder and rectum, carefully dividing the peritoneum to separate these anatomical structures.
A dilated cystic structure is observed replacing the prostate, visible in the surgical field.
During intraoperative cystoscopy, a large opening is visible at the seven o'clock position at the level of the prostatic urethra.
The surgeon visualizes a large cystic sac while negotiating the tract during the robot-assisted urethrocutaneous fistula excision.
The surgeon begins anterior dissection through the space of Retzius, attempting to access the cystic sac that could not be fully dissected from the posterior approach.
The surgeon approaches the cystic sac from the left side of the bladder, incising the endopelvic fascia and carefully dissecting the sac from surrounding anatomical structures.
Stay sutures are applied to improve the visualization of the cystic sac, which is then divided.
During simultaneous cystoscopy, the surgeon identifies the fistulous opening into the urethra, visually confirming the connection between the cystic sac and the urethra.
The surgeon traces the remnant sac using stay sutures and proceeds to resect it, ensuring complete removal from the surrounding tissue.
A surgical team injects methylene blue through the external opening of the fistula, while another team begins dissection from the perianal region, highlighting the fistula tract.
The surgeon delineates the entire fistula tract using methylene blue, followed by complete excision of the tract through a combined abdominal perineal approach.
The surgeon repairs the urethra using Vicryl 3-0 sutures, carefully closing the identified fistulous opening.
The surgeon repairs the defect in the pelvis using StrataFix sutures, while the perineal wound is left open to heal by secondary intention.
